:::{#rem-observed-vs-causal-marginal-risk}

#### Observed vs. causal marginal risk

Both marginal risks are built from the *same* conditional risk function
$\teal{\pi(a, z)} = \Pr(Y = 1 \mid A = a, Z = z)$,
and they differ only in the distribution of $Z$ they average it over:

$$
\ba
\text{observed: } \quad \pi(a)
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)} \mid \red{A = a}}
&&= \int \teal{\pi(a, z)}\, \red{f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)}\, dz
\\
\text{causal: } \quad \pi_a
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}}
&&= \int \teal{\pi(a, z)}\, \red{f_{Z}(z)}\, dz
\ea
$$

The observed marginal risk $\pi(a)$ (@thm-observed-marginal-risk)
weights $\teal{\pi(a, z)}$ by the covariate distribution
$\red{f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)}$ **among those actually exposed to** $A = a$,
so it inherits any association between $A$ and $Z$
(that is, any confounding).

The causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@thm-causal-marginal-risk)
weights $\teal{\pi(a, z)}$ by the **whole population's** covariate distribution
$\red{f_{Z}(z)}$,
which is the *same* for every exposure level $a$;
that common weighting is what makes the causal contrast
$\pi_1 - \pi_0$ an apples-to-apples comparison.

The two coincide,
$\pi(a) = \pi_a$,
exactly when $Z \perp A$ (no confounding),
because then $\red{f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)} = \red{f_{Z}(z)}$.
When $A$ and $Z$ are associated,
the two diverge,
as the numerical example below illustrates (@exm-marginal-point-estimate).

:::
